questions.text: Winston Royce described the Waterfall model in 1970. What was ironic about his paper?
questions.textNL: Winston Royce beschreef het Waterfall model in 1970. Wat was ironisch aan zijn paper?
questions.question: Winston Royce beschreef het Waterfall model in 1970. Wat was ironisch aan zijn paper?
questions.questionEN: Winston Royce described the Waterfall model in 1970. What was ironic about his paper?
options: He actually advocated for Agile working
He himself also warned about the risks of the model
He claimed that requirements never change
He prohibited the use of gates between phases
optionsEN: He actually advocated for Agile working
He himself also warned about the risks of the model
He claimed that requirements never change
He prohibited the use of gates between phases
optionsNL: Hij pleitte juist voor Agile werken
Hij waarschuwde zelf ook voor de risico's van het model
Hij beweerde dat requirements nooit veranderen
Hij verbood het gebruik van gates tussen fasen
questions.explanation: Royce described the sequential model but also warned about its risks. That part of his paper was ignored by many.
questions.explanationNL: Royce beschreef het sequentiële model maar waarschuwde ook voor de risico's ervan. Dat deel van zijn paper werd door velen genegeerd.

questions.text: What is the correct sequence of the six Waterfall phases?
questions.textNL: Wat is de juiste volgorde van de zes Waterfall fasen?
questions.question: Wat is de juiste volgorde van de zes Waterfall fasen?
questions.questionEN: What is the correct sequence of the six Waterfall phases?
options: Design → Requirements → Development → Testing → Deployment → Maintenance
Requirements → Design → Development → Testing → Deployment → Maintenance
Requirements → Development → Design → Testing → Deployment → Maintenance
Design → Development → Requirements → Deployment → Testing → Maintenance
optionsEN: Design → Requirements → Development → Testing → Deployment → Maintenance
Requirements → Design → Development → Testing → Deployment → Maintenance
Requirements → Development → Design → Testing → Deployment → Maintenance
Design → Development → Requirements → Deployment → Testing → Maintenance
optionsNL: Design → Requirements → Development → Testing → Deployment → Maintenance
Requirements → Design → Development → Testing → Deployment → Maintenance
Requirements → Development → Design → Testing → Deployment → Maintenance
Design → Development → Requirements → Deployment → Testing → Maintenance
questions.explanation: The canonical sequence is: Requirements → Design → Development → Testing → Deployment → Maintenance. Each phase produces output that serves as input for the next.
questions.explanationNL: De canonieke volgorde is: Requirements → Design → Development → Testing → Deployment → Maintenance. Elke fase levert output die input is voor de volgende.

questions.text: Statistics from the Requirements lesson show that fixing a requirements error in production is how many times more expensive than fixing it in the requirements phase?
questions.textNL: Statistieken uit de Requirements les tonen dat het fixen van een requirements fout in productie hoeveel keer duurder is dan in de requirements fase?
questions.question: Statistieken uit de Requirements les tonen dat het fixen van een requirements fout in productie hoeveel keer duurder is dan in de requirements fase?
questions.questionEN: Statistics from the Requirements lesson show that fixing a requirements error in production is how many times more expensive than fixing it in the requirements phase?
options: 10x more expensive
25x more expensive
100x more expensive
50x more expensive
optionsEN: 10x more expensive
25x more expensive
100x more expensive
50x more expensive
optionsNL: 10x duurder
25x duurder
100x duurder
50x duurder
questions.explanation: A requirements error that is only discovered in production can cost up to 100x more to fix than if it had been corrected during the requirements phase.
questions.explanationNL: Een requirements fout die pas in productie wordt ontdekt kost tot 100x meer om te fixen dan wanneer hij in de requirements fase was gecorrigeerd.

questions.text: Which principle describes good requirements as Specific, Measurable, Achievable, Relevant and Traceable?
questions.textNL: Welk principe beschrijft goede requirements als Specific, Measurable, Achievable, Relevant en Traceable?
questions.question: Welk principe beschrijft goede requirements als Specific, Measurable, Achievable, Relevant en Traceable?
questions.questionEN: Which principle describes good requirements as Specific, Measurable, Achievable, Relevant and Traceable?
options: MoSCoW prioritisation
SMART principle
IEEE 830 standard
INVEST criteria
optionsEN: MoSCoW prioritisation
SMART principle
IEEE 830 standard
INVEST criteria
optionsNL: MoSCoW prioritering
SMART principe
IEEE 830 standaard
INVEST criteria
questions.explanation: The SMART principle is used in the Requirements lesson to write good requirements: Specific, Measurable, Achievable, Relevant and Traceable.
questions.explanationNL: Het SMART principe wordt in de Requirements les gebruikt om goede requirements te schrijven: Specific, Measurable, Achievable, Relevant en Traceable.

questions.text: What exactly does a Traceability Matrix document?
questions.textNL: Wat documenteert een Traceability Matrix precies?
questions.question: Wat documenteert een Traceability Matrix precies?
questions.questionEN: What exactly does a Traceability Matrix document?
options: The communication structure between stakeholders
The dependencies on the critical path
The link from each requirement to design, code and test cases
The risks per project phase
optionsEN: The communication structure between stakeholders
The dependencies on the critical path
The link from each requirement to design, code and test cases
The risks per project phase
optionsNL: De communicatiestructuur tussen stakeholders
De afhankelijkheden op het kritieke pad
De koppeling van elke requirement naar design, code en testcases
De risico's per projectfase
questions.explanation: A Traceability Matrix tracks where each requirement comes from and where it leads: Business Need → Requirement → Design → Code → Test Case. This aids impact analysis and verification.
questions.explanationNL: Een Traceability Matrix houdt bij waar elke requirement vandaan komt en waar hij naartoe gaat: Business Need → Requirement → Design → Code → Test Case. Dit helpt bij impact analysis en verificatie.

questions.text: In the Design phase you distinguish between High-Level Design and Low-Level Design. What is the difference?
questions.textNL: In de Design fase spreek je over High-Level Design en Low-Level Design. Wat is het verschil?
questions.question: In de Design fase spreek je over High-Level Design en Low-Level Design. Wat is het verschil?
questions.questionEN: In the Design phase you distinguish between High-Level Design and Low-Level Design. What is the difference?
options: High-level is for developers, low-level is for managers
High-level describes system architecture and technology choices; low-level describes components, database schemas and API specifications
High-level is only produced after development; low-level is the upfront blueprint
There is no functional difference; the terms are synonymous
optionsEN: High-level is for developers, low-level is for managers
High-level describes system architecture and technology choices; low-level describes components, database schemas and API specifications
High-level is only produced after development; low-level is the upfront blueprint
There is no functional difference; the terms are synonymous
optionsNL: High-level is voor developers, low-level voor managers
High-level beschrijft systeemarchitectuur en technologiekeuzes; low-level beschrijft componenten, database schema's en API specificaties
High-level wordt pas na development gemaakt; low-level is de blauwdruk vooraf
Er is geen functioneel verschil; het zijn synoniemen
questions.explanation: High-Level Design (System Design) provides the "big picture": architecture, main components and technology choices. Low-Level Design (Detailed Design) provides the detail for developers: database schemas, API specs and algorithms.
questions.explanationNL: High-Level Design (System Design) geeft het "big picture": architectuur, hoofdcomponenten en technologiekeuzes. Low-Level Design (Detailed Design) geeft het detail voor developers: database schema's, API specs en algoritmes.

questions.text: Which type of testing is performed by end users to validate that the system meets the business requirements?
questions.textNL: Welk type testing wordt uitgevoerd door eindgebruikers om te valideren dat het systeem voldoet aan de business requirements?
questions.question: Welk type testing wordt uitgevoerd door eindgebruikers om te valideren dat het systeem voldoet aan de business requirements?
questions.questionEN: Which type of testing is performed by end users to validate that the system meets the business requirements?
options: Integration Testing
System Testing
Regression Testing
User Acceptance Testing (UAT)
optionsEN: Integration Testing
System Testing
Regression Testing
User Acceptance Testing (UAT)
optionsNL: Integration Testing
System Testing
Regression Testing
User Acceptance Testing (UAT)
questions.explanation: UAT (User Acceptance Testing) is performed by end users and is the formal go/no-go validation before the system goes to production.
questions.explanationNL: UAT (User Acceptance Testing) wordt uitgevoerd door eindgebruikers en is de formele go/no-go validatie voordat het systeem naar productie gaat.
